speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Stermer, you were describing the dimensions of the second cave. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We just got in there. And we looked around. And we see corridors. Like a corridor here. Corridor here. It's like routes. If you look on the ground, you don't see no mark. Nobody will see. Not even animals. Nothing. Where you walked, you put your feet, it make a mark like that. Because the-- the-- the ground was soft. It was not completely muddy, but soft. When you touch the stone, well, the stone was like-- like you put some cream on it. Like maybe a [INAUDIBLE] of cream. If you touch it, you made a mark. All the stones like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were afraid to get lost. We start making marks. We start-- we had a big rope we tied on. And we start walking. And we walk. We see corridor here. Corridor there. And we walk.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as we walk up a little hill, a stone-- because it was little stones all over. A stone roll off. And I hear splash in water. Hey, water.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I remember-- this, I remember correct. I was the one that slide down. And I put my two hands in the water like that. And I taste it. And I say, sweet water. So good water. Hey, we have water here. We be able to be here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we walk. We walk further. And we see a big lake. We'll have water. Rooms. I said, oh, here's a place that we can-- we'll be able to bring our family. We walked around a-- a few hours there. And we say now-- we look in some places that danger. Here can a stone fall. We had to find a place should be safe. So we find one corridor. Round like a train. And about 200 feet long and about 15 feet-- 12, 15 feet wide. But the ground was going like that, like a-- wild, everything. So we say, I think this is the place that we going to set up our quarters. And we came back. We went back home. Brought the good news that we have a place. A good cave. Water inside. And it's very hard to-- to come-- somebody should come in there. So on the 5th of May at night, we load up everything. My brother still have that little horse and the wagon. And we came there, and we start bringing in the stuff. It was very hard to bring in, pieces by pieces. And we all came inside. The horse and the wagon, they just give it away. And everybody's in.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1943, 5 of May. Now, we brought in-- my mother had a hard time to come in there. It was very hard. We had to clean through it. We had to, with the shovel, to-- to-- to clean through the ground, and she had to-- to crawl and to crawl. We had even there a-- a lady, the Mrs.-- from the Dodek family, I think, about 70 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was your mother at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was born in-- in 1888. So she was about 50--

speaker: interviewee
text: 56, 55, something like that. So we came inside, and we went, and we find this place where we set up. And we were working all day at that time to clean up the place. Now we have to make some beds, how to-- where to sleep. We brought in a few boards, but it was not enough. At night, we got out in the forest. Because we brought with us tools. We brought shovels, big. Saws, hammers. We brought the tools because we knew that we need this here. We brought in some wood. And we set up bunks for the families. For everybody. But we were-- all the six of us. Not my older sister-- my younger sister-- my older sister Chana was not with us. And we set up where to sleep. OK. Now, Nissen went out and he brought in-- it was-- it was like the Passover also for the Gentiles. So he went there to-- to a place, to the priest. To the priest in Bilcze Zlote. There was-- they used to bring to the priest those-- those like [POLISH] challos. And they had so much, they used to feed horses. My brother, with the-- the-- the guy that he was by the horses, he knew him. And he gave him about seven, eight challos. And he brought them in the cave. It was like-- and we didn't have enough food. And we start to think what can we do that we should be survive here, because can't just go out. There was no bakery. There was nowhere to go to buy bread. There was nowhere to buy nothing. And we still have eight people. You have to eat something.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the beginning--

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS] --my father bought some flour. But you see, we need-- we need something-- we need to make something, flour ourself. Because grain was easier to get. Grain, you could get, but not-- so we build ourself a-- we had a-- a-- how you call it. A grinding stone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two stone. We have to build this. It calls in Ukrainian a [UKRAINIAN]. A little mill. So where we can get this here? So there was a friend, a Ukrainian guy, that he was supposed to-- we send him in a place. My brother paid him the money.

speaker: interviewee
text: He had to bring to his house about 100 kilo of flour. This was 100 kilo of diamonds, we had this value, today. And my-- we came to pick it up. He says when he was driving from this place, from this place, the-- the Germans took it away from him. We knew it's a lie.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my brother says, no. He's not taking this answer. He came at night. Me and him. Didn't go to the door. He opened just the window. And he came in inside. He was sleeping.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says to him, give us back the flour. And he kept-- he had a piece of-- like a piece of pipe, steel pipe, my brother. And he said-- he said, give us back the flour. There was no police. There was no Germans in this area. And you have it. Give us back. And as he turn around, in the corner stays there one of this here. Anyway, he give us back half of it. The other half, it disappeared. Took it back. Then when we need the stone to make this here, he says, we going to go to him. We-- he owe us something. And at night, my brother went there. I was not there. I think he went with my brother Sam, because I-- I went another place. And this guy had a stone. It's about two feet by-- a round stone. In the middle is about four inch hole with a handle, and when you turn this-- and he brought this to the cave. Now, we have this. Good. We need another one. So I went outside, and I knew in a place there were a lot of stones. And I picked a nice flat stone. And I brought it inside. We took wood in a corner, completely in a corner, and I build a stand. Like-- like a vanity. Exactly a vanity. I build the stand. Took that stone. It had to be rough. Chip to make it rough. And with a piece of tin, had to be round, around. And in one place, we just let it-- I think to get-- the flour should come out. And we put up the stone. We made the stand. We started-- we put a little bit grain. Hey, flour is come out. Good. Flour. And we made this here. We made this flour. Now, we had to-- we set up a-- I'm a little bit going too fast. We set up to sleep. Now we have to set up a place where to cook. Because we remember in the other cave that the-- the-- the smoke shouldn't kill us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So went away. This a big route. We went away and we find a place-- come a place. And there's a lake. And near this lake is a stone, a bit like a table. And by the lake, we see like-- like a-- a few stones to go down. I says, who made this here? Somebody like made for us this here.

speaker: interviewee
text: We said, this is going to be a good place to cook. And my mother, she always-- she always had a head. She knew what she needed to go there. She brought with her a copper pot about-- kept a pail of water like. Copper. Used to make jam with this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at-- from an iron ornamental, a three leg-- a three leg-- how you call it? It stand like-- it was about five inches high with three little legs. You put this on the top, and you put some wood underneath, and this was the cooking. With the handle. So we say-- we set up our cooking there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, we need wood. So we went outside in the forest at night. We used to go out, 12 people. This was the most dangerous thing. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We went out. Two guys used to cut, and you had to cut pieces of wood no more than five-- four, five feet long, because otherwise you couldn't be-- wouldn't able to go in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So two guys cut trees. And we used to cut up about 10 trees at night, at least. About 10 inches of feet-- thick. Tall trees. Hard wood. We brought in the-- the trees. Was like that. A team. Two guys.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two guys cut the trees with the saw. Cut pieces. One guy or two guys were standing with axes, clearing the branches. The other went-- carry inside were young boys. They took it away. And inside, we have to cut these trees. Small pieces like for kindling, how you call it. Small pieces of wood. And this was the wood for the cooking. And this was very danger to go out at night. And you made so much noise. And this was going on for six weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you preparing yourself for the winter, or for a long stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. We knew that what winter, it was-- but you see, there was-- the fields were very good. There was potatoes. But in June were not-- nothing yet. At the end of June, July, it started. So we were there. Nobody knew that he are there. One day, we want to go out. We come out. We can't go out. The local people find out that we are there. And they came. And they fill up that ravine, what I told you. 40 feet high and-- they just fill it up completely.

speaker: interviewer
text: With what?

speaker: interviewee
text: With earth. They dug all around, and they fill it up, and they say they-- they Jewish. They-- they being smelt. All the Jewish are going to die there. But we had a little experience from the other cave, and we knew that we have to-- what happen if this happens? We start to preparing another exit. We walking around all over and say stuff. We have nothing. We can't find. But in this little room, in this little room where we just came in, in the valley, was like-- but-- in the corner, everything is stone. But we see there is a soft spot. We start digging there. And we-- we made a-- we were closed, and we made a-- another exit. And it came out in a very good place. It came with something under a tree and a side that we could-- we could cover it. But you had to cover it from inside. It was a place like a round place, a tree on the side. And this was lower a little bit. And we came up with a hole there. So I made another store-- another frame, like in the first cave. Another frame without a nail. Has to click together like that. And I put the frame and a piece of tin we had cover-- a piece of tin to cover up that frame. And we put a-- a-- near there a pile of soft earth like sand. And with the side near the frame, we pushed out-- pushed our hand with a little bit, and I tried to cover it from inside. Because now we-- we are-- everybody's inside, and we cover this here. And they show me, you have to do it to the left. Do it to the right. And it was good. And we were going with this here for a full week like that. The other place we came out was terrible to look. Full. Terrible. Full. Here, we were going like that. One day, we came. We-- one evening when we go out because we changed the day for the night. We had-- night we have to go out, and the day we used to sleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: We coming in and we see that frame, everything is in. They discovered us again. Now-- what we going to do now? How can we go out? Who knows what's there? So-- so we build a little-- a little puppet. How you call it? A-- we-- we put-- we made like a piece of wood across, and this here. And we dressed up. Made a man. Put him on the hat with the-- dressed up like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we try to push him out, if somebody's not going to hit. We see nobody touches this guy. We push him out. It's good. Anyway, I think my brother may push out his head, does he know what is there.

speaker: interviewer
text: We came out. Nobody was there. So we go to that [POLISH] and tell him. [POLISH] says we know. The-- those shepherds were playing. They discovered us again. They discover you again. What can we do now? So we are-- we have no choice. We are-- we're going for a few weeks. We were going for a while with this place. We are going. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This was summertime, and the fields was potatoes, corn, beans, beans. Was-- it was good. We were going around, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and we used to bring in the food, and everything was good. One day, I-- I was sleeping next to my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Says, you know something? We didn't have a piece of meat for maybe more than two years. Maybe a year already. More than a year. Was in the cave. Let's go somewhere to steal sheep. Say, where shall we go? We will go to Bilcze Zlote. There is-- on the end of the village, there's a farmer. We go there. We give him some stuff. He didn't want to give us back, this guy. But he has sheep.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came out at night. We-- we usually used to go out about 10, 11 o'clock at night. And we walk over with a bag, with a knife, with a piece of pipe. Something in the hand. And we walk and we come there to this guy, and he had a lot of sheep. But he had two big dogs on the-- We went around where the sheeps are-- where the sheep. And the dogs are barking, and we are lying there quiet. And they made for them a-- they fenced them up, you know, like with some wood that you could not-- you can't open this here. You can't do nothing. We had a knife. Start slowly, slowly. We cut a hole. But I tried to catch a-- they run away. I pull-- I went inside through the hole. I catch one. I touch it. Skinny. I let it go. I grab a fat one. I give it to my brother. He covers here. Puts in his bag. We cut on the field, cut the throat, and we came in with a sheep. It was like a feast. We came with the sheep. Now, next time we go to another guy. He had some stuff of ours. And we went to-- not far from there. To get a piece of bread, something. He didn't-- he didn't-- we knock the door. He didn't want to answer. And I look around. It stays a bag full of those-- the farmers, they're cleaning where they make from linen very strong stuff. Big shirts, they wear, and pants. Very strong, like-- I think it's made of linen. So very strong stuff. I say to my brother, don't wake him. Wait a second. Because we came into the cave, no, nothing. What I wear-- what I had on me, that I-- that-- that what I had. And there, my pants, every day. This was-- I had to fix this. I had to fix this because of the stone and this here. Say, don't wake him. And it was about 10 or 12 pair. If we wouldn't have this here, we had to go naked. We brought this in. And this was so good. We put it in on the-- and big shirts and pants, and the old stuff, that then on the top. So this was very good with this here. And this was going on like that. We used to go at night. Potatoes. We go and corn.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the summer now?

speaker: interviewee
text: The summer. This is June, July. Yeah. When we came in, we had to wash something. You didn't dry nothing. So was my mother put it. And we were sleeping in it. Cover it up with something. He was sleeping for dry it. And we-- we were-- was very good to sleep there. The air was always the same, the same temperature. In summertime, used to go bare feet. No shoes. There was-- first we didn't have good shoes, what we had, and it was very good. Because you walked, it was very quiet. See, because if you wearing shoes, you make noise like that. We were very quiet. And my mother-- my mother knew exactly what-- how the moon is outside. She-- she-- she went by the Jewish calendar in her-- in her head.

speaker: interviewer
text: When she came in, she knew there is so many days in the month. And she would kept track. She used to tell us, boys, today's a full moon. Be very careful. I say, Mama. You are-- how do you know that's a full moon? You see-- you were outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: I thought she is joking. Come on. It's a full moon. So she tell us with-- today's a full moon. She kept it. And it was going on like that. And we said one time, you know, a guy came. He says, you know that there's a guy is marrying up a daughter. And when they marry a daughter there, or make a wedding, the neighbors and the people, friends, bring always a chicken, ducks, goose. They bring there. Said, he must have a lot of that stuff. So OK, I take my brother. Me and Nissen. The oldest one, me, and another guy is not alive. They kill him after the war. Josel Dodek. And we go to this guy. Josel goes to the door just in case that the farmer wants to go out, he should let us know, or should-- and I go with Nissen to the barn where those ducks are. I go first. And Nissen has the bag. Soon we open the door. Those ducks making noise. And my brother says, get round. I say, hold the bag. Hold it. And I lie down. I didn't see nothing. I lie down.

speaker: interviewer
text: One in the bag. Second. Filled up a full bag with ducks. Grab it. And we run to the cave. He come into the cave. We touch those ducks. They were so skinny, it was only feathers. What shall we do with them? Wait a second. We made a little place for them. We brought in from outside some hay. We put them there. And there was already corn on the field. And we fed them, you know, force-- how they call it? Force feeding.

speaker: interviewee
text: Force feeding. We were feeding. And before Rosh Hashanah, we had the ducks. We didn't have a [INAUDIBLE]. My brother was a good [INAUDIBLE]. And we-- we had-- it was about 15 ducks. All the families divided, and we had a-- we had a [POLISH]. How you call it, that fat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we cook-- we cook potatoes, and we cook the-- and it was-- so this was a very good-- a good job. Then later on, come fall. He says, you know, we never had a little bit jam for year-- for a long time. How can we make some jam? So we know that's a one place there-- the-- those sugar beets. White sugar beets are going there always. So it was a meeting. A board meeting. [LAUGHS] I talk to my brother. We are going-- going to go for those beets.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother Sam with Karl, they going to go for apples in the forest. And-- and there-- there's a-- a guy that he has very good apples. And at night, about 11 o'clock, two bag. I take two bags. The shovel, with my brother. And we go. And we had to cross a main road. And the police wasn't no more than a-- than a kilometer from there, the Ukrainian police. But we went down. And we came down to the beets. There is beets. But it was-- I think a flood. It's-- every beet was so hard to take out, like it was in cement. Because it was rain, a flood, and then it dried out. But we didn't go away without beets. We filled up two bags of beets. And we started walking back. [DRINKS] My brother, of course, a big bag. A smaller bag. And I tell to my brother, I can't. They're big-- those beets make me hole in my bag. They're making holes. I can't. He says, throw out one.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I threw out-- he didn't throw out nothing. So I threw out one. And I go further, say, Nissen. I can't carry. They make-- they-- they making me holes in my bag. Say, throw out another one.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, we came with the beets. And they came with the apples. And we made marmalade. We had-- my mother-- we-- we had marmalade. So this is the way the-- now--

speaker: interviewer
text: Aside from the dangerous--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the danger that you were courting each time you had to go out--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were inside the cave, did you hear shooting, or did you hear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. It was so deep and so far away. It was-- this was a-- a very deep cave. Now we had to-- we started-- we need another exit. We need one. So we walked around and we find another place-- find a place. And we started working under. But-- and we came a little bit high. And this place was something by nature. One place was-- one-- one part was a stone, a big stone right through way up. One side was blue clay, very heavy earth. And one part gravel. Those three-- and we start going up. We didn't have nothing to-- to-- to brace it. We went through this about 20 feet, and then it start already good earth. And we start going up. And I see I am very high. All the same, we were experienced cave-- cave guys. And I was so high there with-- very high. And I see that my light, my little light, is keeping-- there was no more oxygen there. They-- they keep-- out-- I put down again. And I find already a piece of wood, that I know that we are soon going to be out. But while I'm up there, I hear noise. And then they say, hey. You are closed up already. This ground in the gravel start coming down and close up. I didn't even know that I was on the top that everything is closed. And they-- there were five, six guys there. They clean it out. And says, come down. I came down. And we went back. We went back to sleep, like to eat and to sleep. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But we were-- I went back. We-- we were eating there twice a day. My sister, Yetta, she was the go-to cook. Two soups a day. One about 7 o'clock in the morning. One at 7-- but two times a day, that's all what we had. A little bit of soup. Plate of soup. I used to say to my mother, if that soup could last at least half an hour, it will be good. We came back. We had the soup. We went to sleep. Now-- next day, we have to go to work, back in there. We came there. And there's so much of this blue stuff, this gravel. Mountains came down. We start cleaning, and we cleaning. And more, we took away. More came. We couldn't do nothing. Now, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one day we came and this hole is filled up. The second exit. Again, they fill it up.

speaker: interviewer
text: But we have to go out. 38 people have to eat. Shall I stop? So what can we do? What I think, we have to try the first one. To open the first one. Because they filled it up. The ground settled already. It was about three, four months. So we opened the first one. And we say like that. Now there is potatoes on the field. And the farmers, when they take out the potatoes, they-- first they put them in big piles. And then they take them home.
